title: Purification of a monitored qubit: exact path-integral solution
authors: Matheus M. R. Poltronieri Martins, Henrique Santos Lima
abstract: We investigate the purification dynamics of a single qubit under continuous in time monitoring. By employing a collisional model framework where the system interacts sequentially with ancillary qubits, we describe the conditioned evolution of the density matrix through a stochastic master equation. We show that for initial mixed states, the dynamics reduce to a multiplicative Langevin equation for a single scalar parameter representing the state's purity. This stochastic process is solved exactly using the Onsager-Machlup path integral formalism, allowing us to derive the full probability distribution for the qubit's trajectories. Our analytical results reveal that purification is characterized by a dynamical crossover from a diffusion dominated regime to a measurement dominated regime, visible in the emergence of a bimodal state distribution. The analytical solutions are in strong agreement with numerical simulations, providing a robust theoretical benchmark for the study of information extraction in monitored quantum systems.
summary: This paper provides an exact analytical solution for the purification of a single qubit under continuous monitoring. By using a path-integral approach, the authors demonstrate how a qubit transitions from a mixed state to a bimodal distribution of pure states. This work serves as a robust theoretical benchmark for studying information extraction in monitored quantum systems.
main_problem: The paper investigates the purification dynamics of a single qubit under continuous-in-time monitoring, specifically seeking an exact analytical solution for the full probability distribution of the qubit's trajectories.
main_result: The authors derive an exact closed-form solution for the probability distribution of the qubit's state and purity using the Onsager-Machlup path-integral formalism, revealing a dynamical crossover from a diffusion-dominated regime to a measurement-dominated regime characterized by bimodality.
method: The study employs a collisional model framework, maps the stochastic master equation to a multiplicative Langevin equation, and uses the Onsager-Machlup path-integral approach and Girsanov's theorem to solve the dynamics.
model_or_system: A single qubit initially in a mixed state interacting sequentially with a stream of ancillary probe qubits in a collisional model, described by a stochastic master equation in the continuous monitoring limit.
key_observables: State coordinate (q), purity (tau), effective purification rate (Omega), and the probability distributions of these quantities.
important_parameters: Measurement rate (eta), time (t), and the crossover threshold (eta*t = 1).
main_assumptions: The system is limited to a single qubit, meaning it cannot exhibit many-body measurement-induced phase transitions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the collisional model schematic; Figure 2 displays individual qubit trajectories; Figure 3 illustrates the transition of the action landscape from one to two minima; Figure 4 compares analytical and numerical results for the effective-rate distribution and mean purity.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the collisional model and stochastic master equation, applies a mathematical transformation to solve the Langevin equation via path integrals, validates the analytical results against numerical simulations, and discusses the physical implications of the observed dynamical crossover.
why_interesting: It provides an exact analytical benchmark for understanding how continuous measurement extracts information and drives purification, which is fundamental to the study of open quantum systems and measurement-induced dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Collisional implementation of continuous monitoring. A system qubit S interacts during a short time interval dt with one ancillary probe prepared in the state |ϕ⟩through the weak unitary U = ZS(θ)UCNOT(θ). The ancilla is then projectively measured and discarded, so that each collision extracts only partial information about the system. In the scaling limit θ = √ηdt ≪1, the sequence of weak updates converges to a Markovian conditioned evolution, yielding the stochastic master equation used throughout the paper.
FIG. 2. Individual trajectories q(t) versus ηt generated from Eq.(4). Notice that, in the present time scale, q is naturally bounded in ±1.

FIG. 3. Left: solutions of the extremal condition Ω/η = tanh(Ωt), Eq. (13), plotted as a function of (ηt)−1. The nonzero branches appear for ηt > 1 and approach Ω= ±η at long times. Right: action S(Ω, t) from Eq. (12) for increasing times. The central minimum at Ω= 0 becomes a local maximum and is replaced by two symmetric minima, providing the origin of the bimodal purification distribution.

FIG. 4. Left: probability density PΩ(Ω, t) versus Ω/η from Eq. (11) for increasing values of the dimensionless time ηt. The initially single-peaked distribution broadens and evolves toward two dominant branches at finite Ω, signaling the onset of measurement-selected trajectories. Right: mean purity E[τ] = ⟨τ⟩obtained by averaging τ(q) over 100, 000 Euler–Maruyama trajectories of Eq. (3) (solid blue line) and from the analytical expression in Eq. (17) (red dashed line) versus ηt.

FIG. 5. Full trajectory distributions for the monitored qubit. Top: probability density Pq(q, t) for ηt = 0.1, 0.5, 2.0, comparing numerical trajectory histograms (orange) with the analytical result of Eq. (14) (solid blue line). Bottom: corresponding purity distribution Pτ(τ, t) obtained from Eq. (16). At short times the state remains concentrated near the maximally mixed value q = 0 and τ = 1/2, whereas at later times the weight shifts toward |q| ≃1 and τ ≃1, demonstrating measurement-induced purification.
